questions.questionText: A nuclear reactor starts producing a radioactive nuclide X from t = 0, at a constant rate of α per second. Each decay of X produces energy E0, which is utilized to heat a liquid of mass m and specific heat s. Assuming no heat loss from the liquid and taking λ as the decay constant of X, the rate of increase in the temperature of the liquid is :
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
Let $\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{t})$ be the number of active nuclei of the radioactive substance X present in the reactor at any time t.

The net rate of change of the number of nuclei ($\frac{\mathrm{d}N}{\mathrm{d}t}$) is of concurrent processes, the production of nuclei at a constant rate, which is $+\alpha$ and the decay of nuclei which is proportional to the number of nuclei present, given by $-\lambda N$.

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}N}{\mathrm{d}t}=\alpha-\lambda N$$

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}N}{\alpha-\lambda N}=\mathrm{d}t$$

At $t=0$, the reactor just starts producing the nuclide, so the initial number of nuclei is $N=0$.

At time $t$, let the number of nuclei be $N$:

$$\int\limits_{0}^{N} \frac{\mathrm{d}N}{\alpha-\lambda N}=\int\limits_{0}^{t} \mathrm{d}t$$

For integral $\int\limits_{0}^{N} \frac{\mathrm{d}N}{\alpha-\lambda N}$, on substitution $u=\alpha-\lambda N \rightarrow \mathrm{d}u=-\lambda\,\mathrm{d}N \rightarrow \mathrm{d}N=-\frac{\mathrm{d}u}{\lambda}$

$$\int\limits_{0}^{N} \frac{\mathrm{d}N}{\alpha-\lambda N}=\int \frac{1}{u}\left(-\frac{\mathrm{d}u}{\lambda}\right)=-\frac{1}{\lambda}\ln|u|=-\frac{1}{\lambda}\ln|\alpha-\lambda N|$$

$$=\left[-\frac{1}{\lambda}\ln(\alpha-\lambda N)\right]_{0}^{N}=t$$

$$\ln(\alpha-\lambda N)-\ln(\alpha)=-\lambda t$$

Using the logarithmic identity $\ln A-\ln B=\ln\frac{A}{B}$:

$$\ln\left(\frac{\alpha-\lambda N}{\alpha}\right)=-\lambda t$$

$$\frac{\alpha-\lambda N}{\alpha}=e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$\alpha-\lambda N=\alpha e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$\lambda N=\alpha-\alpha e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$\lambda N=\alpha\left(1-e^{-\lambda t}\right)$$

The activity $A(t)$ of a radioactive sample is the total number of decays occurring per second.

$$A(t)=\lambda N$$

$$A(t)=\alpha\left(1-e^{-\lambda t}\right)$$

Each decay releases a fixed amount of thermal energy $E_0$. Therefore, the total thermal energy released per unit time (the power input to the liquid, $\frac{dQ}{dt}$) is

$$\frac{dQ}{dt}=A(t)\cdot E_0=\alpha E_0\left(1-e^{-\lambda t}\right)$$

When heat energy $Q$ is supplied to a liquid of mass $m$ and specific heat capacity $s$, the relationship between the heat supplied and the resulting temperature rise $\Delta T$ is given by

$$Q=ms\,\Delta T$$

For an infinitesimal change over time $dt$,

$$dQ=ms\,dT$$

$$\frac{dQ}{dt}=ms\frac{dT}{dt}$$

$$\frac{dT}{dt}=\frac{1}{ms}\left(\frac{dQ}{dt}\right)$$

$$\frac{dT}{dt}=\frac{1}{ms}\left[\alpha E_0\left(1-e^{-\lambda t}\right)\right]$$

$$\frac{dT}{dt}=\frac{\alpha E_0}{ms}\left(1-e^{-\lambda t}\right)$$

Therefore, the rate of increase of temperature of liquid is $\frac{\alpha E_0}{ms}\left(1-e^{-\lambda t}\right)$. Thus, the correct option is (A).

questions.questionText:
A metal wire of cross-sectional area 0.5 mm2 and length 100 m is connected across a battery of e.m.f. 2 V and internal resistance 1 Ω. The density, atomic mass and electrical conductivity of the metal are 6.35 × 103 kg m−3, 63.5 gm/mole and 2 × 108 mho m−1, respectively. Assuming one conduction electron per atom of the metal, the drift velocity (in mm s−1) of the electrons in the wire is:

[Take Avogadro’s number as 6 × 1023 and charge of the electron as 1.6 × 10−19 C.]
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
For a conductor of uniform cross-sectional area A and length L, the number of conduction electrons per unit

volume (number density) is n.

If the electrons drift with an average velocity $v_d$, electric current I is defined as the rate of flow of charge:

$$I = \frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta t} = n e A v_d$$

The drift velocity $v_d$ is,

$$v_d = \frac{I}{n e A}$$

It is given that each atom contributes exactly one conduction electron. Therefore, the number density of

electrons n is equal to the number density of atoms in the metal.

Let the mass density of the metal be $\rho$ and its molar mass be $M$.

The volume of one mole of the metal is:

$$V_{\mathrm{mole}} = \frac{M}{\rho}$$

One mole of any substance contains Avogadro's number $(N_A)$ of atoms.

Since there is 1 conduction electron per atom, one mole contains $N_A$ conduction electrons. The number of

electrons per unit volume is:

$$n = \frac{N_A}{V_{\mathrm{mole}}} = \frac{\rho N_A}{M}$$

The density is $\rho = 6.35 \times 10^3\ \mathrm{kg\ m^{-3}}$

The molar mass is $M = 63.5\ \mathrm{gm/mole} = 63.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{kg/mole}$

Avogadro's number is $N_A = 6 \times 10^{23}\ \mathrm{atoms/mole}$

Substituting values into the expression for n:

$$n = \frac{(6.35 \times 10^3) \times (6 \times 10^{23})}{63.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$n = \frac{6.35}{63.5} \times 10^3 \times 10^3 \times 6 \times 10^{23}$$

$$n = 0.1 \times 6 \times 10^{29} = 6 \times 10^{28}\ \mathrm{electrons\ m^{-3}}$$

The resistance R of a wire depends on its length L, cross-sectional area A, and electrical conductivity $\sigma$. By

definition, resistivity $\rho_e$ is the reciprocal of conductivity $\left(\rho_e = \frac{1}{\sigma}\right)$.

$$ \mathrm{R}=\frac{\rho_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{~L}}{\mathrm{~A}}=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{\sigma \mathrm{~A}} $$

The length of wire is $\mathrm{L}=100 \mathrm{~m}$

Electrical conductivity is $\sigma=2 \times 10^8 \mathrm{mhom}^{-1}$

The cross-sectional area is $\mathrm{A}=0.5 \mathrm{~mm}^2=0.5 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^2$

Substituting these values:

$$ \mathrm{R}=\frac{100}{\left(2 \times 10^8\right) \times\left(0.5 \times 10^{-6}\right)} $$

$$ \mathrm{R}=\frac{100}{2 \times 0.5 \times 10^2}=\frac{100}{1 \times 100}=1 \Omega $$

The wire is connected to a battery of electromotive force (e.m.f.) $\mathrm{V}=2 \mathrm{~V}$ and internal resistance $\mathrm{r}=1 \Omega$.

By Ohm's law applied to the total closed loop, the total circuit current I is :

$$I = \frac{V}{R + r}$$

Substituting the values of $R$ and $r$:

$$I = \frac{2}{1 + 1} = \frac{2}{2} = 1\ \mathrm{A}$$

Now substituting the values of current $I$, number density $n$, electron charge $e$, and cross-sectional area $A$ into the drift velocity equation:

$$v_d = \frac{I}{n e A}$$

The current is $I = 1\ \mathrm{A}$

The number density is $n = 6 \times 10^{28}\ \mathrm{m}^{-3}$

The electronic charge is $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \mathrm{C}$

And area of cross-section is $A = 0.5 \times 10^{-6}\ \mathrm{m}^2$

$$v_d = \frac{1}{(6 \times 10^{28}) \times (1.6 \times 10^{-19}) \times (0.5 \times 10^{-6})}$$

$$v_d = \frac{1}{6 \times 1.6 \times 0.5 \times 10^{28-19-6}}$$

$$v_d = \frac{1}{4.8 \times 10^3}$$

$$v_d = \frac{1}{4800}\ \mathrm{m\ s}^{-1}$$

$$v_d = \frac{10^3}{4800}\ \mathrm{mm\ s}^{-1} = \frac{1000}{4800}\ \mathrm{mm\ s}^{-1} = \frac{1}{4.8}\ \mathrm{mm\ s}^{-1}$$

$$v_d \approx 0.2083\ \mathrm{mm\ s}^{-1}$$

Therefore, the drift velocity of electron in given conductor is $0.2083\ \mathrm{mm\ s}^{-1}$. Hence, the correct option is (C).

questions.questionText: Two charges $Q_1 = q$ and $Q_2 = mq$ are placed at the points $P_1(a, b)$ and $P_2(ma, mb)$, respectively, in the $XY$ plane, where $a, b \neq 0$ and $m \neq 0, 1$. If $V_1$ is the potential at a point in the $XY$ plane due to charge $Q_1$ and $V_2$ is the potential at that point due to charge $Q_2$. Correct statement(s) for the points at which $|V_1| = |V_2|$ is/are :
options: For $m = -1$, locus of these points is $ax + by = 0$.
For $m = 2$, the locus of these points is a circle of radius $\frac{2}{3}\sqrt{a^2+b^2}$ centered at $\left(\frac{2}{3}a, \frac{2}{3}b\right)$
For $m = -2$, the locus of these points is a circle of radius $2\sqrt{a^2+b^2}$ centered at $(2a, 2b)$
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
Let an arbitrary point in the XY plane be $P(x,y)$.

The electrostatic potential $V$ at a distance $r$ from a point charge $Q$ is derived from the work done per unit charge bringing it from infinity to that point:

$$V=\frac{kQ}{r}$$

where $k=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}$.

The distance $r_1$ from the point $P_1(a,b)$ to $P(x,y)$ is given by the distance formula:

$$r_1=\sqrt{(x-a)^2+(y-b)^2}$$

The potential $V_1$ at $P(x,y)$ due to $Q_1=q$ is:

$$V_1=\frac{kq}{\sqrt{(x-a)^2+(y-b)^2}}$$

The distance $r_2$ from the point $P_2(ma,mb)$ to $P(x,y)$ is:

$$r_2=\sqrt{(x-ma)^2+(y-mb)^2}$$

The potential $V_2$ at $P(x,y)$ due to $Q_2=mq$ is:

$$V_2=\frac{k(mq)}{\sqrt{(x-ma)^2+(y-mb)^2}}$$

For the locus of points satisfying the condition $|V_1|=|V_2|$:

$$\left|\frac{kq}{\sqrt{(x-a)^2+(y-b)^2}}\right|=\left|\frac{kmq}{\sqrt{(x-ma)^2+(y-mb)^2}}\right|$$

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{(x-a)^2+(y-b)^2}}=\frac{m}{\sqrt{(x-ma)^2+(y-mb)^2}}$$

Squaring both sides

$$\frac{1}{(x-a)^2+(y-b)^2}=\frac{m^2}{(x-ma)^2+(y-mb)^2}$$

$$ (x-ma)^2+(y-mb)^2=m^2\left[(x-a)^2+(y-b)^2\right] $$

For $m=-1$

$$ (x-(-1)a)^2+(y-(-1)b)^2=(-1)^2\left[(x-a)^2+(y-b)^2\right] $$

$$ (x+a)^2+(y+b)^2=(x-a)^2+(y-b)^2 $$

$$ x^2+2ax+a^2+y^2+2by+b^2=x^2-2ax+a^2+y^2-2by+b^2 $$

$$ 2ax+2by=-2ax-2by $$

$$ 4ax+4by=0 \rightarrow ax+by=0 $$

Thus, option (A) is correct.

For $m=2$

$$ (x-2a)^2+(y-2b)^2=2^2\left[(x-a)^2+(y-b)^2\right] $$

$$ (x-2a)^2+(y-2b)^2=4\left[(x-a)^2+(y-b)^2\right] $$

$$ (x^2-4ax+4a^2)+(y^2-4by+4b^2)=4(x^2-2ax+a^2+y^2-2by+b^2) $$

$$ x^2-4ax+4a^2+y^2-4by+4b^2=4x^2-8ax+4a^2+4y^2-8by+4b^2 $$

$$ (4x^2-x^2)+(-8ax+4ax)+(4a^2-4a^2)+(4y^2-y^2)+(-8by+4by)+(4b^2-4b^2)=0 $$

$$ 3x^2-4ax+3y^2-4by=0 $$

$$ x^2-\frac{4}{3}ax+y^2-\frac{4}{3}by=0 $$

The standard circle form $(x^2+y^2+2gx+2fy+c=0)$:

$$ \left(x-\frac{2}{3}a\right)^2-\left(\frac{2}{3}a\right)^2+\left(y-\frac{2}{3}b\right)^2-\left(\frac{2}{3}b\right)^2=0 $$

$$ \left(x-\frac{2}{3}a\right)^2+\left(y-\frac{2}{3}b\right)^2=\frac{4}{9}a^2+\frac{4}{9}b^2=\frac{4}{9}(a^2+b^2) $$

This is a circle equation of the form $(x-x_c)^2+(y-y_c)^2=R^2$:

Where the centre is $(x_c,y_c)=\left(\frac{2}{3}a,\frac{2}{3}b\right)$

And radius is $R=\sqrt{\frac{4}{9}(a^2+b^2)}=\frac{2}{3}\sqrt{a^2+b^2}$

Thus, option (B) is correct.

For $m=-2$

$(x-(-2) a)^2+(y-(-2) b)^2=(-2)^2\left[(x-a)^2+(y-b)^2\right]$

$(x+2 a)^2+(y+2 b)^2=4\left[(x-a)^2+(y-b)^2\right]$

$$x^2+4ax+4a^2+y^2+4by+4b^2=4x^2-8ax+4a^2+4y^2-8by+4b^2$$

$$3x^2 - 12ax + 3y^2 - 12by = 0$$

$$x^2 - 4ax + y^2 - 4by = 0$$

$$(x - 2a)^2 - 4a^2 + (y - 2b)^2 - 4b^2 = 0$$

$$(x - 2a)^2 + (y - 2b)^2 = 4a^2 + 4b^2 = 4(a^2 + b^2)$$

This is also a circle with centre $(2a, 2b)$ and radius

$$\sqrt{4(a^2 + b^2)} = 2\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}$$

Thus, option (C) is correct.

For $m = -3$

$$(x + 3a)^2 + (y + 3b)^2 = 9[(x - a)^2 + (y - b)^2]$$

$$x^2 + 6ax + 9a^2 + y^2 + 6by + 9b^2 = 9x^2 - 18ax + 9a^2 + 9y^2 - 18by + 9b^2$$

$$8x^2 - 24ax + 8y^2 - 24by = 0$$

$$x^2 - 3ax + y^2 - 3by = 0$$

This also represents a circle, not a straight line as given by $3bx + 3ay = 0$. Thus, option (D) is incorrect.

Therefore, the correct options are (A), (B) and (C).

questions.questionText: Consider an electric dipole comprising two charges $+q$ and $-q$ each with mass $m$, separated by a fixed distance $d$ and initially at rest with its dipole moment pointing along $\hat{\imath}$. A uniform electric field $E \hat{\jmath}$ is turned on at time $t = 0$ and it is turned off at $t = t_f$, when the dipole moment makes an angle $\theta_f$ with $\hat{\imath}$. Neglecting any sources of energy loss, correct option(s) is/are :
options: The center of mass of the dipole is deflected towards $\hat{\jmath}$ in the presence of the field.
If the magnitude of the final angular velocity $\omega_f = \sqrt{\frac{2qE}{md}}$, then $\theta_f = \frac{\pi}{6}$.
If $\theta_f = \pi/3$, then the change in kinetic energy of the dipole is given by $2\sqrt{3}\ qEd$.
For $\theta_f = \pi/4$, the dipole rotates around its center of mass with a constant angular velocity after $t > t_f$.
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
An electric dipole consists of two equal and opposite charges separated by a distance vector $\vec{d}$. The electric dipole moment vector $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}$ is defined as :

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}=\mathrm{q} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{~d}} $$

When the dipole is placed in a uniform electric field $\vec{E}=E \hat{\jmath}$. The force experienced by a point charge $Q$ in an electric field is given by $\vec{F}=Q \vec{E}$

The force on the positive charge $+q$ is:

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{+}=(+\mathrm{q})(\mathrm{E} \hat{\jmath})=\mathrm{qE} \hat{\jmath} $$

The force on the negative charge -q is:

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{-}=(-\mathrm{q})(\mathrm{E} \hat{\jmath})=-\mathrm{qE} \hat{\jmath} $$

The total external force $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\mathrm{ext}}$ acting on the dipole system is the vector sum of these individual forces:

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\mathrm{ext}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{+}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{-}=\mathrm{qE} \hat{\jmath}+(-\mathrm{qE} \hat{\jmath})=0 $$

By Newton's second law for a system of particles, the acceleration of the center of mass $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}_{\mathrm{cm}}$ is related to the net external force by:

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\mathrm{ext}}=\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{total}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}_{\mathrm{cm}} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}_{\mathrm{cm}}=0 $$

Since the dipole is initially completely at rest, its initial center of mass velocity is zero. Because the net external acceleration remains zero at all times, the center of mass must remain stationary at its initial position. It does not deflect in any direction. Thus, option (A) is incorrect.

The torque $\vec{\tau}$ exerted by a uniform electric field on a dipole is given by the vector cross product:

$$ \vec{\tau}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} $$

Initially, at $t=0, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}$ points along $\hat{1}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}$ points along $\hat{\mathrm{j}}$.

As the dipole rotates, let $\theta$ be the instantaneous angle that the dipole moment vector makes with the positive x -axis (î).

The magnitude of this restoring torque tending to align the dipole with the electric field along $\hat{\jmath}$ is:

$$ \tau=|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}||\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}| \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right)=\mathrm{pE} \cos \theta=\mathrm{qdE} \cos \theta $$

The moment of inertia I of the dipole rotating about its center of mass (the midpoint of the fixed distance d , since both charges have identical mass m ) is:

$$I = m\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2 + m\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{md^2}{4} + \frac{md^2}{4} = \frac{1}{2}md^2$$

Using work-energy theorem for rotational systems the work done by the torque equals the change in the rotational kinetic energy of the system:

$$W = \int\limits_{0}^{\theta_f} \tau \, d\theta = \Delta K_{\mathrm{rot}}$$

Substituting the expression for torque:

$$W = \int\limits_{0}^{\theta_f} (qdE \cos \theta) \, d\theta = qdE\,[\sin \theta]_{0}^{\theta_f} = qdE \sin \theta_f$$

Since the dipole starts from rest, its initial kinetic energy is zero ($K_i = 0$):

$$\Delta K_{\mathrm{rot}} = K_f - 0 = \frac{1}{2}I\omega_f^2$$

$$\frac{1}{2}I\omega_f^2 = qdE \sin \theta_f$$

Substituting the expression for the moment of inertia $I = \frac{1}{2}md^2$:

$$\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{2}md^2\right)\omega_f^2 = qdE \sin \theta_f$$

$$\frac{1}{4}md^2\omega_f^2 = qdE \sin \theta_f \rightarrow \omega_f^2 = \frac{4qE}{md}\sin \theta_f$$

$$\omega_f = \sqrt{\frac{4qE}{md}\sin \theta_f}$$

For $\omega_f = \sqrt{\frac{2qE}{md}}$

$$\sqrt{\frac{4qE}{md}}\sin \theta_f = \sqrt{\frac{2qE}{md}}$$

$$\frac{4qE}{md}\sin \theta_f = \frac{2qE}{md}$$

$$4\sin \theta_f = 2 \rightarrow \sin \theta_f = \frac{2}{4} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$\theta_f = \frac{\pi}{6}$$

Thus, option (B) is correct.

When $\theta_f = \frac{\pi}{3}$ using work-energy theorem,

$$\Delta K = qdE\sin \theta_f$$

$$\Delta K = qdE\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right) = qdE\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}qEd$$

Thus, Option (C) is incorrect.

At $t=t_f$, the electric field is completely turned off $(\vec{E}=0)$.

When the electric field becomes zero, the electric torque acting on the dipole falls to zero

$$ \vec{\tau}=\vec{p} \times 0=0 $$

According to the rotational form of Newton's second law:

$$ \vec{\tau}=\vec{I} \alpha \Rightarrow \vec{\alpha}=0 $$

Since the angular acceleration $\vec{\alpha}$ is zero for all times $\mathrm{t}>\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{f}}$, the angular velocity must remain constant at its final value $\omega_{\mathrm{f}}$. Thus, Option (D) is correct.

Therefore, the correct options are (B) and (D).

questions.questionText: A beam of polychromatic light passes through a thin prism of prism angle $6^{\circ}$. The refractive index of the material of the prism varies with wavelength $(\lambda)$ as $n(\lambda) = \alpha \lambda + \frac{\beta}{\lambda^2}$, where $\alpha = 3\ \mu m^{-1}$ and $\beta = 0.096\ \mu m^2$. If $\lambda_{min}$ is the wavelength at which the angle of minimum deviation $D_m$ is smallest, then the correct value of $D_m$ at $\lambda_{min}$ is :
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
For a light ray passing through a prism with a small prism angle $A$, the angles of incidence and emergence are also small.

By Snell's law at the first interface:

$$\sin i_1 = n \sin r_1$$

Using the small-angle approximation $\sin \theta \approx \theta$ in radians):

$$i_1 \approx n r_1$$

Similarly, at the second interface:

$$\sin e = n \sin r_2 \rightarrow e \approx n r_2$$

The total angle of deviation $D$ produced by a prism is given by the sum of deviations at both faces:

$$D = (i_1 - r_1) + (e - r_2) = (i_1 + e) - (r_1 + r_2)$$

From the geometry of a prism, the interior angles relate to the prism angle $A$ via:

$$r_1 + r_2 = A$$

Substituting this and the small-angle approximations into the total deviation formula:

$$D \approx (n r_1 + n r_2) - A = n(r_1 + r_2) - A$$

$$D = nA - A = (n - 1)A$$

For a thin prism, the deviation angle remains nearly uniform across varying small angles of incidence, meaning the angle of minimum deviation $D_m$ depends on the refractive index of the prism at particular wavelength of light.

$$D_m(\lambda) = (n(\lambda) - 1)A$$

To find the wavelength where $D_m$ reaches its lowest value, we find the minimum of the function $D_m(\lambda)$ with respect to $\lambda$.

The given function for the refractive index is:

$$n(\lambda) = \alpha \lambda + \frac{\beta}{\lambda^2} = \alpha \lambda + \beta \lambda^{-2}$$

Differentiating $n$ with respect to $\lambda$:

$$\frac{dn}{d\lambda} = \frac{d}{d\lambda}(\alpha \lambda + \beta \lambda^{-2}) = \alpha - 2\beta \lambda^{-3} = \alpha - \frac{2\beta}{\lambda^3}$$

To find the critical point where the function reaches its minimum,

$$\frac{dn}{d\lambda} = 0$$

$$\alpha - \frac{2\beta}{\lambda_{\min}^3} = 0$$

$$\frac{2\beta}{\lambda_{\min}^{3}} = \alpha$$

$$\lambda_{\min}^{3} = \frac{2\beta}{\alpha}$$

$$\lambda_{\min} = \left(\frac{2\beta}{\alpha}\right)^{1/3}$$

To verify that this critical point represents a true minimum, we find the second derivative:

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} n}{\mathrm{d}\lambda^{2}} = \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}\lambda}\left(\alpha - 2\beta\lambda^{-3}\right) = 6\beta\lambda^{-4} = \frac{6\beta}{\lambda^{4}}$$

Since constants $\beta$ and $\lambda$ are positive, $\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} n}{\mathrm{d}\lambda^{2}} > 0$, $\lambda_{\min}$ gives the absolute minimum value for the refractive index and subsequently the smallest deviation.

Substituting the given numerical constants into the expression for $\lambda_{\min}^{3}$:

$\alpha = 3\mu\ \mathrm{m}^{-1}$, $\beta = 0.096\mu\ \mathrm{m}^{2}$

So,

$$\lambda_{\min}^{3} = \frac{2 \times 0.096}{3} = \frac{0.192}{3} = 0.064\mu\ \mathrm{m}^{3}$$

$$\lambda_{\min} = (0.064)^{1/3} = 0.4\mu\ \mathrm{m}$$

The value of the refractive index $n$ at this specific wavelength $\lambda_{\min} = 0.4\mu\ \mathrm{m}$:

$$n(\lambda_{\min}) = \alpha\lambda_{\min} + \frac{\beta}{\lambda_{\min}^{2}}$$

$$n(\lambda_{\min}) = 3 \times 0.4 + \frac{0.096}{(0.4)^{2}}$$

$$n(\lambda_{\min}) = 1.2 + \frac{0.096}{0.16}$$

$$n(\lambda_{\min}) = 1.2 + 0.6 = 1.8$$

Using the thin prism deviation equation

$$D_{m} = (n - 1)A$$

$$D_{m} = (1.8 - 1) \times 6^{\circ}$$

$$D_{m} = 0.8 \times 6^{\circ} = 4.8^{\circ}$$

Therefore, the correct option is (B).

questions.questionText: Consider two isosceles prisms 1 and 2 with prism angles $A_1$ and $A_2$ and refractive indices $n_1$ and $n_2$, respectively, as shown in the figure. The faces $a_1 b_1$ and $a_2 b_2$ are parallel to each other and perpendicular to the mirror $M$. If a ray of light is incident on the face $a_1 c_1$ and emerges from the face $a_2 c_2$, then the correct statement(s) is/are :
options: If both the prisms are at minimum deviation condition, then $\dfrac{n_2}{n_1} = \dfrac{\sin\left(\dfrac{A_1}{2}\right)}{\sin\left(\dfrac{A_2}{2}\right)}$.
If prism 2 is at minimum deviation condition, then $\sin i_1 = n_2 \sin \left(\dfrac{A_2}{2}\right)$ is always true.

If both the prisms 1 and 2 are thin and are at minimum deviation condition with angles of deviation $\delta_{m1}$ and $\delta_{m2}$, respectively, then $\theta = \dfrac{\delta_{m1}}{2(n_1 - 1)} + \dfrac{\delta_{m2}}{2(n_2 - 1)}$.
If prism 1 is at minimum deviation condition, then $\sin i_2 = n_1 \sin \left(\dfrac{A_1}{2}\right)$ is always true.
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
The prism angle be A and the refractive index be n .

The sum of the interior refraction angles at the first and second surfaces ( $r_1$ and $r_2$ ) is equal to the prism angle

$$ \mathrm{r}_1+\mathrm{r}_2=\mathrm{A} $$

At the condition of minimum deviation $\left(\delta=\delta_{\mathrm{m}}\right)$, the path of the light ray through the prism becomes completely symmetric with respect to the two refracting faces. Therefore, the angle of incidence equals the angle of emergence ( $\mathrm{i}=\mathrm{e}$ ),

So, $\mathrm{r}_1=\mathrm{r}_2=\mathrm{r}$

$$ \Rightarrow r+r=A \Rightarrow r=\frac{A}{2} $$

Applying Snell's law at the first refracting surface:

$$ \sin \mathrm{i}=\mathrm{n} \sin \mathrm{r}_1=\mathrm{n} \sin \left(\frac{\mathrm{~A}}{2}\right) . \tag{i} $$

The light ray as it exits Prism 1, reflects off the horizontal mirror M , and enters Prism 2.

The vertical faces $a_1 b_1$ and $a_2 b_2$ are given as parallel to each other and perpendicular to the horizontal mirror M.

Since the faces $a_1 b_1$ and $a_2 b_2$ are perpendicular to the mirror, any line perpendicular to these vertical faces is exactly parallel to the mirror M .

Let the ray emerge from the face $a_1 b_1$ at an angle of emergence $e_1$ relative to the normal of face $a_1 b_1$. Because the face normal is horizontal (parallel to M ), the angle that the emerging ray makes with the horizontal line is exactly $\mathrm{e}_1$.

This ray travels downward and strikes the horizontal mirror M . By the law of reflection, the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection relative to the normal of the mirror.

In terms of the angle made with the horizontal mirror surface:

Angle of incidence with horizontal $=\mathrm{e}_1$

Angle of reflection with horizontal $=\mathrm{e}_1$

The reflected ray travels upward toward the vertical face $a_2 b_2$ of Prism 2 .

Since face $a_2 b_2$ is parallel to face $a_1 b_1$, its surface normal is also horizontal. Therefore, the ray approaching face $a_2 b_2$ makes an angle $e_1$ with the horizontal line. This means the angle of incidence $i_2$ at the face $a_2 b_2$ must be equal to $\mathrm{e}_1$ :

$$ \mathrm{i}_2=\mathrm{e}_1 $$

If prism 1 is operating at its minimum deviation condition, then its angle of incidence ( $\mathrm{i}_1$ ) equals its angle of emergence ( $\mathrm{e}_1$ ).

Using equation (i).

$$ \sin \mathrm{e}_1=\mathrm{n}_1 \sin \left(\frac{\mathrm{~A}_1}{2}\right) $$

Since $\mathrm{i}_2=\mathrm{e}_1$

$$ \sin \mathrm{i}_2=\mathrm{n}_1 \sin \left(\frac{\mathrm{~A}_1}{2}\right) $$

So, according to the geometry of the parallel faces and the mirror, making it always true. Thus, Option (D) is correct.

If prism 2 is operating at its minimum deviation condition, then its angle of incidence ( $\mathrm{i}_2$ ) equals its angle of emergence ( $\mathrm{e}_2$ ).

$$ \sin \mathrm{i}_2=\mathrm{n}_2 \sin \left(\frac{\mathrm{~A}_2}{2}\right) $$

Since $\mathrm{i}_2=\mathrm{e}_1$,

$\sin \mathrm{e}_1=\mathrm{n}_2 \sin \left(\frac{\mathrm{~A}_2}{2}\right)$.

If only prism 2 is at minimum deviation, the relationship between $\mathrm{e}_1$ and $\mathrm{i}_1$ depends only on the specific properties of prism 1 (prism angle $A_1$ and refractive index $n_1$ ). Thus, $\sin i_1=n_2 \sin \left(\frac{A_2}{2}\right)$ is not always true. Thus, option (B) is incorrect.

If both prisms are simultaneously operating at minimum deviation, then:

From prism 1: $\sin \mathrm{e}_1=\mathrm{n}_1 \sin \left(\frac{\mathrm{~A}_1}{2}\right)$

From prism 2: $\sin i_2=n_2 \sin \left(\frac{A_2}{2}\right)$

Since $\mathrm{i}_2=\mathrm{e}_1$, their sines must be identical $\sin \mathrm{i}_2=\sin \mathrm{e}_1$ :

$$ n_1 \sin \left(\frac{A_1}{2}\right)=n_2 \sin \left(\frac{A_2}{2}\right) $$

$$ \frac{\mathrm{n}_2}{\mathrm{n}_1}=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{\mathrm{A}_1}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{\mathrm{A}_2}{2}\right)} $$

Thus, option (A) is correct.

For thin prisms ( $\mathrm{A} \ll 1$ radian) under small angles of incidence, the minimum deviation expression using the small-angle approximation $\sin \theta \approx \theta$ :

$$ \delta_{\mathrm{m} 1}=\left(\mathrm{n}_1-1\right) \mathrm{A}_1 \Rightarrow \mathrm{~A}_1=\frac{\delta_{\mathrm{m} 1}}{\mathrm{n}_1-1} $$

$$ \delta_{\mathrm{m} 2}=\left(\mathrm{n}_2-1\right) \mathrm{A}_2 \Rightarrow \mathrm{~A}_2=\frac{\delta_{\mathrm{m} 2}}{\mathrm{n}_2-1} $$

The base face $a_1 b_1$ makes an angle of $\frac{A_1}{2}$ with the face $a_1 c_1$ since the prisms are isosceles.

Similarly, face $a_2 b_2$ makes an angle of $\frac{A_2}{2}$ with face $a_2 c_2$.

Because the vertical lines $a_1 b_1$ and $a_2 b_2$ are parallel, the total angle between the outer faces $a_1 c_1$ and $a_2 c_2$ when extended to meet at the top is simply the sum of their individual inclinations relative to the vertical line:

$$\theta = \frac{A_1}{2} + \frac{A_2}{2}$$

Substituting the expressions for $A_1$ and $A_2$ from the thin-prism approximation

$$\theta = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\delta_{m1}}{n_1 - 1}\right) + \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\delta_{m2}}{n_2 - 1}\right)$$

$$\theta = \frac{\delta_{m1}}{2(n_1 - 1)} + \frac{\delta_{m2}}{2(n_2 - 1)}$$

Thus, option (C) is correct.

Therefore, the correct options are (A), (C), and (D).

questions.questionText: A particle of mass m, and angular momentum ℓ is moving in a circular orbit of radius r0 under the influence of an attractive force $\vec{F}(r)=-\frac{k}{r^2} \hat{r}$. Keeping its angular momentum unchanged, the particle is displaced radially by a small distance $\delta r \ll r_0$, due to which its radial distance varies periodically. The corresponding time period is :
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
The particle of mass $m$ moving in a plane under a central force field $\vec{F}(r)=F(r) \hat{r}$.

In polar coordinates $(r, \theta)$, the position vector is $r = r\hat{r}$.

Differentiating the position vector with respect to time gives the velocity vector:

$$\vec{v} = \left(\frac{dr}{dt}\right) \hat{r} + r\frac{d\theta}{dt} \hat{\theta}$$

Differentiating once more gives the acceleration vector:

$$\vec{a} = \left(\frac{d^2 r}{dt^2} - r\left(\frac{d\theta}{dt}\right)^2\right) \hat{r} + \left(r\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + 2\left(\frac{dr}{dt}\right)\right) \hat{\theta}$$

By Newton's second law, $\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$. Since the force is purely radial, the transverse component of acceleration must be zero, which means the angular momentum $l$ is a conserved quantity.

$$l = m r^2 \omega \Rightarrow \frac{d\theta}{dt} = \frac{l}{m\,r^2}$$

Using Newton's second law for the radial component:

$$m\left(\frac{d^2 r}{dt^2} - r\left(\frac{d\theta}{dt}\right)^2\right) = F(r)$$

$$m\left(\frac{d^2 r}{dt^2} - r\left(\frac{l}{m\,r^2}\right)^2\right) = F(r)$$

$$m\frac{d^2 r}{dt^2} - \frac{l^2}{m\,r^3} = F(r)$$

$$m\frac{d^2 r}{dt^2} = \frac{l^2}{m\,r^3} + F(r)$$

The given attractive force is $F(r)=-kr^2$, the equation becomes:

$$m\frac{d^2r}{dt^2}=\frac{l^2}{mr^3}-kr^2$$

For a perfect circular orbit of constant radius $r_0$, the radial distance does not change over time,

$$\frac{dr}{dt}=0 \text{ and } \frac{d^2r}{dt^2}=0.$$

From the radial equation of motion:

$$\frac{l^2}{mr_0^3}-\frac{k}{r_0^2}=0$$

$$\frac{l^2}{mr_0^3}=\frac{k}{r_0^2} \Rightarrow r_0=\frac{l^2}{mk}$$

When the particle is given a small radial displacement $\delta\, r$ from its equilibrium position $r_0$. Let its instantaneous radius be:

$$r=r_0+\delta\, r$$

Taking the second time derivative, since $r_0$ is a constant:

$$\frac{d^2r}{dt^2}=\frac{d^2}{dt^2}(r_0+\delta\, r)=\frac{d^2\delta r}{dt^2}$$

$$m\frac{d^2\delta r}{dt^2}=\frac{l^2}{m(r_0+\delta r)^3}-\frac{k}{(r_0+\delta r)^2}$$

$$m\frac{d^2\delta r}{dt^2}=\frac{l^2}{m r_0^3\left(1+\frac{\delta r}{r_0}\right)^3}-\frac{k}{r_0^2\left(1+\frac{\delta r}{r_0}\right)^2}$$

$$m\frac{d^2\delta r}{dt^2}=\frac{l^2}{m r_0^3}\left(1+\frac{\delta r}{r_0}\right)^{-3}-\frac{k}{r_0^2}\left(1+\frac{\delta r}{r_0}\right)^{-2}$$

$$m\frac{d^2\delta r}{dt^2}=\frac{l^2}{m r_0^3}\left(1+\frac{\delta r}{r_0}\right)^{-3}-\frac{k}{r_0^2}\left(1+\frac{\delta r}{r_0}\right)^{-2}$$

Since $\delta\, r \ll r_0$, we can use the binomial expansion $(1+x)^n \approx 1+nx$ for $|x| \ll 1$:

$$m\frac{d^2\delta r}{dt^2}\approx \frac{l^2}{m r_0^3}\left(1-3\frac{\delta r}{r_0}\right)-\frac{k}{r_0^2}\left(1-2\frac{\delta r}{r_0}\right)$$

$$m\frac{d^2\delta r}{dt^2}\approx \left(\frac{l^2}{m r_0^3}-\frac{k}{r_0^2}\right)-\left(\frac{3l^2}{m r_0^3}-\frac{2k}{r_0^2}\right)\delta r$$

From the equilibrium condition $\frac{l^2}{m r_0^3}-\frac{k}{r_0^2}=0.$

$$ \mathrm{m} \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 \delta \mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{dt}^2} \approx=-\left(\frac{3 \mathrm{l}^2}{\mathrm{mr}_0^4}-\frac{2 \mathrm{k}}{\mathrm{r}_0^3}\right) \delta \mathrm{r} $$

Substituting $\mathrm{l}^2=\mathrm{mkr}_0$ from the equilibrium condition

$$ \frac{3\left(\mathrm{mkr}_0\right)}{\mathrm{m} \mathrm{r}_0^4}-\frac{2 \mathrm{k}}{\mathrm{r}_0^3}=\frac{3 \mathrm{k}}{\mathrm{r}_0^3}-\frac{2 \mathrm{k}}{\mathrm{r}_0^3}=\frac{\mathrm{k}}{\mathrm{r}_0^3} $$

The standard simple harmonic motion (SHM) form $\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 \delta \mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{dt}^2}=-\omega^2 \delta \mathrm{r}$ :

$$ \mathrm{m} \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 \delta \mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{dt}^2}=-\frac{\mathrm{k}}{\mathrm{r}_0^3} \delta \mathrm{r} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 \delta \mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{dt}^2}=-\left(\frac{\mathrm{k}}{\mathrm{mr}_0^3}\right) \delta \mathrm{r} $$

Thus, the angular frequency of the radial oscillations is:

$$ \omega=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{k}}{\mathrm{mr}_0^3}} $$

The relationship for the time period T of an oscillation is:

$$ \mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \pi}{\omega}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{mr}_0^3}{\mathrm{k}}} $$

Using $\mathrm{r}_0=\frac{\mathrm{l}^2}{\mathrm{mk}}$ :

$$ \mathrm{T}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{~m}}{\mathrm{k}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{l}^2}{\mathrm{mk}}\right)^3}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{~m}}{\mathrm{k}} \frac{\mathrm{l}^6}{\mathrm{~m}^3 \mathrm{k}^3}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{l}^6}{\mathrm{~m}^2 \mathrm{k}^4}}=\frac{2 \pi \mathrm{l}^3}{\mathrm{mk}^2} $$

Therefore, the correct option is (A).

questions.questionText:
Ten moles of an ideal monoatomic gas, initially in state $\boldsymbol{a}$ at atmospheric pressure and temperature $T_a=27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, is enclosed in a metal cylinder of volume $V_0$ fitted with a frictionless piston. The gas is suddenly compressed to state $\boldsymbol{b}$ with volume $V_0 / 3$. Now, keeping the piston stationary, the cylinder is submerged in a water bath of temperature $11^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ until the gas reaches the temperature of the water bath, which is denoted as state $\boldsymbol{c}$. Finally, while still in the water bath, the piston is brought slowly to its initial position, which is denoted as state $\boldsymbol{f}$. If $R$ is universal gas constant, then the correct option(s) is/are :

[Given: $9^{1 / 3}=2.08$ ]
options: The schematic P-V diagram of the processes described above is :
The change in internal energy in going from state a to b is $4860R$.
The net change in the internal energy in the whole process is $-240R$.
The pressure and temperature of the state b are $2.08$ times the atmospheric pressure and $624\,K$, respectively.
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
When a gas is compressed suddenly in a cylinder, there is no sufficient time for heat exchange to take place between the gas and its surroundings $\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{a} \rightarrow \mathrm{b}}=0$. Therefore, this process is adiabatic.

The relation between temperature (T) and volume (V) for an adiabatic process

$$T V^{ u-1} = ext{constant}$$

where $\gamma = \dfrac{C_p}{C_v}$ is the adiabatic constant.

For an ideal monoatomic gas, $C_v = \dfrac{3}{2}R$ and $C_p = \dfrac{5}{2}R$, so:

$$\gamma = \dfrac{5}{3}$$

$$\gamma - 1 = \dfrac{5}{3} - 1 = \dfrac{2}{3}$$

For state a the temperature is given as $T_a = 27^{\circ}\mathrm{C} = 27 + 273 = 300\ \mathrm{K}$

The volume of gas at state a is $V_a = V_0$ and at state b it is $V_b = \dfrac{V_0}{3}$

Applying the adiabatic condition $T_a V_a^{\gamma-1} = T_b V_b^{\gamma-1}$:

$$300 \times (V_0)^{2/3} = T_b \left(\dfrac{V_0}{3}\right)^{2/3}$$

$$T_b = 300 \times (3)^{2/3} = 300 \times (9)^{1/3}$$

Using the given value $(9)^{1/3} = 2.081$

$$T_b = 300 \times 2.081 = 624.3\ \mathrm{K} \approx 624\ \mathrm{K}$$

Using the relation between pressure and volume for adiabatic process, $P V^{\gamma} = \text{constant}$:

$$P_b = P_a \left(\dfrac{V_a}{V_b}\right)^{\gamma} = P_a (3)^{5/3} = P_a \times 3 \times 3^{2/3} = P_a \times 3 \times 2.081 = 6.243\,P_a$$

So, the pressure at state b is approximately 6.24 times the atmospheric pressure, not 2.08 times. Thus, option (D) is incorrect.

The change in internal energy $(\Delta U_{a\to b})$:

$$\Delta U_{a\to b} = n C_v (T_b - T_a)$$

Given $n = 10$ moles and $C_v = \dfrac{3}{2}R$:

$$ \Delta U_{a \rightarrow b}=10 \times\left(\frac{3}{2} R\right) \times(624.3-300) $$

$$ \Delta U_{a \rightarrow b}=15 R \times 324.3=4864.5 R \approx 4860 R$$

Thus, option (B) is correct.

It is given that the piston is kept stationary in the process $\mathrm{b} \rightarrow \mathrm{c}$, which means the volume remains constant $\left(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{c}}=\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{\mathrm{V}_0}{3}\right)$, making it an isochoric process. The gas cools until it reaches the water bath temperature:

$$ \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{c}}=11^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=11+273=284 \mathrm{~K} $$

For the process $\mathrm{c} \rightarrow \mathrm{f}$ the piston is moved slowly while remaining immersed in the water bath, ensuring the gas maintains thermal equilibrium with the reservoir. This is an isothermal process at $\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{c}}=284 \mathrm{~K}$.

The volume is brought back to its initial position:

$$ V_f=V_0 $$

$a \rightarrow b$ the volume decreases from $V_0$ to $\frac{V_0}{3}$ along a steep adiabatic curve.

$\mathrm{b} \rightarrow \mathrm{c}$ the volume stays constant at $\frac{\mathrm{V}_0}{3}$ while pressure falls vertically as temperature drops from 624 K to 284 K .

$\mathrm{c} \rightarrow \mathrm{f}$ the volume expands from $\frac{\mathrm{V}_0}{3}$ back to $\mathrm{V}_0$ along a less steep isothermal curve at 284 K .

Thus, option (A) is correct.

Internal energy is a state function, which means its total change depends on the initial and final temperatures of the system, irrespective of the path taken:

$$ \Delta \mathrm{U}_{\text {net }}=\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{a}}=\mathrm{nC}_{\mathrm{v}}\left(\mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{a}}\right) $$

Temperature at state a is $\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{a}}=300 \mathrm{~K}$ and $\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=284 \mathrm{~K}$ :

$$ \begin{gathered} \Delta U_{\text {net }}=10 \times\left(\frac{3}{2} R\right) \times(284-300) \\ \Delta U_{\text {net }}=15 R \times(-16)=-240 R \end{gathered} $$

Thus, option (C) is correct.

Therefore, the correct options are (A), (B), and (C).

questions.questionText: In a vacuum chamber, a particle of charge $1\ \mu C$ and mass $1\ \mathrm{mg}$ is projected with a velocity $(\hat{i} + 2\hat{j})\ \mathrm{ms}^{-1}$ from the $XZ$ plane at time $t = 0$ in an electric field of $1\hat{i}\ \mathrm{Vm}^{-1}$. At $t = 0.2\ s$, the electric field is switched off and a magnetic field of $6\hat{j}\ \mathrm{T}$ is switched on. The acceleration due to gravity is $-10\hat{j}\ \mathrm{ms}^{-2}$. Correct option(s) is/are :
options: The vertical distance of the particle from the $XZ$ plane at $t = 0.3\ s$ is $15\ \mathrm{cm}$.
The vertical distance of the particle from the $XZ$ plane at $t = 0.4\ s$ is $10\ \mathrm{cm}$.
The radius of the trajectory of the particle for $t > 0.2\ s$ is $20\ \mathrm{cm}$.
The particle will be in the $XZ$ plane at $t = 0.35\ s$.
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
During this first interval $(t=0 \rightarrow t=0.2 \mathrm{~s})$, the particle experiences a uniform electric field $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=1 \hat{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{Vm}^{-1}$ and a uniform gravitational field $\vec{g}=-10 \hat{\mathrm{j}} \mathrm{m} \mathrm{s}^{-2}$.

The charge on particle is, $\mathrm{q}=1 \mu \mathrm{C}=10^{-6} \mathrm{C}$

The mass of the particle is, $\mathrm{m}=1 \mathrm{mg}=10^{-6} \mathrm{~kg}$

The net force acting on the particle is the vector sum of the electric force and gravitational force:

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\mathrm{net}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\mathrm{E}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\mathrm{g}}=\mathrm{q} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}+\mathrm{m} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{~g}} $$

Using Newton's second law, $\vec{a}=\frac{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\mathrm{net}}}{\mathrm{m}}$ :

$$ \vec{a}=\frac{q \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}}{m}+\vec{g} $$

Substituting the value $\frac{\mathrm{q}}{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{10^{-6}}{10^{-6}}=1 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=1(1 \hat{\mathrm{i}})-10 \hat{\mathrm{j}}=(1 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-10 \hat{\mathrm{j}}) \mathrm{m} \mathrm{s}^{-2}$

So, the acceleration components are, $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{x}}=1 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-2} ; \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{y}}=-10 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-2} ; \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{z}}=0 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-2}$

The initial velocity at $\mathrm{t}=0$ is given as $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_0=1 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\jmath} \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$.

Since the accelerations are constant during this interval, we can use the equations of kinematic motion $\vec{v}=\vec{u}+ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{at}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{s}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{ut}}+\frac{1}{2} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{at}}{ }^2$ to find the velocity and position at $\mathrm{t}=0.2 \mathrm{~s}$.

Velocity components at $\mathrm{t}=0.2 \mathrm{~s}$ :

$$ \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{t}=1+(1)(0.2)=1.2 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1} $$

$\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{y}}=\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{y}}+\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{t}=2+(-10)(0.2)=2-2=0 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$

$$ \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{z}}=\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{z}}+\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{z}} \mathrm{t}=0+0=0 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1} $$

Thus, at the exact moment the electric field is turned off, the velocity vector is:

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}(0.2)=1.21 \hat{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1} $$

Position components at $\mathrm{t}=0.2 \mathrm{~s}$ :

The particle starts from the XZ plane, which means its initial vertical position is $\mathrm{y}_0=0$.

$$ y(0.2)=y_0+u_y t+\frac{1}{2} a_y t^2=0+(2)(0.2)+\frac{1}{2}(-10)(0.2)^2 $$

$$ \mathrm{y}(0.2)=0.4-5(0.04)=0.4-0.2=0.2 \mathrm{~m}=20 \mathrm{~cm} $$

At $t=0.2 \mathrm{~s}$, the electric field is turned off, and a magnetic field $\vec{B}=6 \hat{\mathrm{j}} T$ is switched on.

Now, the forces acting on the particle are gravity and the magnetic Lorentz force:

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\mathrm{net}}=\mathrm{m} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{~g}}+\mathrm{q}(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}) $$

Using Newton's second law of motion, the acceleration of the particle is,

$$ \vec{a}=\vec{g}+\frac{q}{m}(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})=-10 \hat{\jmath}+1(\vec{v} \times 6 \hat{k})=-10 \hat{\jmath}+6(\vec{v} \times \hat{k}) $$

At $t=0.2 \mathrm{~s}^{+}$(just a moment after replacing electric field with magnetic field, the velocity is $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}=1.2$ î:

$$ \vec{a}=-10 \hat{\jmath}+6(1.2 \hat{\imath} \times \hat{\jmath})=-10 \hat{\jmath}+6(1.2 \hat{k})=(-10 \hat{\jmath}+7.2 \hat{k}) \mathrm{m} \mathrm{~s}^{-2} $$

As the direction and magnitude of gravitational force remains constant (vertically downward $-10 \hat{\mathrm{j}} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ ). The magnetic force is along positive $Z$-axis and before turning the magnetic field on the velocity is along $X$-axis.

As the acceleration is perpendicular to the velocity vector, the particle will start moving in a circular orbit of radius r . So, the circular path particle will be in XZ plane with accelerated motion along Y -axis due to acceleration due to gravity.

At $\mathrm{t}=0.3 \mathrm{~s}$ where 0.2 s was spent in electric field and rest 0.1 s in magnetic field.

Along vertical direction ( $Y$-axis) for $0.1 \mathrm{~s}(\mathrm{t}=0.3 \mathrm{~s}), \mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{y}}=0 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}, \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{y}}=-10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}, \mathrm{y}_0=\mathrm{y}(0.2)=20 \mathrm{~cm}=$ 0.2 m .

Using kinematic equation along Y ,

$\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{y}_0+\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{b}}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{b}}^2$

$\mathrm{y}=0.2+0 \times 0.2+\frac{1}{2} \times(-10)(0.2)^2=(0.2-0.2) \mathrm{m}=0 \mathrm{~m}$

Which means at $\mathrm{t}=0.4 \mathrm{~s}$ the particle is again at XZ plane.

Hence, the vertical distance of the particle from the XZ plane at $\mathrm{t}=0.4 \mathrm{~s}$ is 0 cm . Thus, option B is incorrect.

When the particle is in circular motion (in XZ plane), the required centripetal force acting on it $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{c}}=7.2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$.

If the radius of circular path is $r$, then $a_c=\frac{v^2}{r}$

$$ \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{c}}=\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{B}} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{v}^2}{\mathrm{r}}=7.2 \Rightarrow \mathrm{r}=\frac{1.2^2}{7.2}=0.2 \mathrm{~m}=20 \mathrm{~cm} $$

Therefore, the curvature radius of the path by the magnetic field interaction is 20 cm . Thus, option (C) is correct.

At $t=0.35 \mathrm{~s}$ where 0.2 s was spent in electric field and rest 0.15 s in magnetic field.

Along vertical direction (Y-axis) for $0.15 \mathrm{~s}(\mathrm{t}=0.35 \mathrm{~s}), \mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{y}}=0 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}, \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{y}}=-10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}, \mathrm{y}_0=\mathrm{y}(0.2)= 20 \mathrm{~cm}=0.2 \mathrm{~m}$.

Using kinematic equation along Y ,

$$ \mathrm{y}=\mathrm{y}_0+\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{b}}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{b}}^2 $$

$$ \mathrm{y}=0.2+0 \times 0.15+\frac{1}{2} \times(-10)(0.15)^2=(0.2-0.1125) \mathrm{m}=1.8875 \mathrm{~m} $$

Hence, the vertical distance of the particle from the XZ plane at $\mathrm{t}=0.35 \mathrm{~s}$ is 1.89 m . So, at $\mathrm{t}=0.35 \mathrm{~s}$ the particle is moving in a circle which is parallel to XZ plane but at a distance of 1.89 m . Thus, option D is incorrect.

Therefore, the correct options are (B) and (C).

questions.questionText:
A container of height 2 m , length 2 m and breadth 1 m is made of insulating vertical walls and two large area horizontal metal plates ( $\mathrm{M}_1$ and $\mathrm{M}_2$ ) which extend far beyond the vertical walls in all directions. The container is partitioned into two equal chambers with a thin insulating vertical wall. The partition wall contains a small hole of cross-sectional area $\sqrt{10} \mathrm{~cm}^2$ near its bottom edge. Initially the hole is closed and the left chamber of the container is completely filled with a liquid of dielectric constant $\epsilon_r=15$ and the right chamber is empty ( $\epsilon_r=1$ ). At time $t=0$, the hole is opened and the liquid flows from the left chamber to the right chamber. In both the chambers, the space above the liquid has $\epsilon_r=1$ and is maintained at atmospheric pressure. The schematic of the container at a time $t>0$ is shown in the figure.

[Given : acceleration due to gravity is $10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$.]

The difference in the capacitance (in F) between the metal plates at $t=0$ and that at $t=500 \mathrm{~s}$ is $(8-n) \epsilon_0$, where $\epsilon_0$ is the permittivity of free space. The value of $n$ is :
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
The two large parallel horizontal metal plates ( $\mathrm{M}_1$ and $\mathrm{M}_2$ ) form a parallel-plate capacitor system.

The total height of the container is $\mathrm{H}=2 \mathrm{~m}$, the total length is $\mathrm{L}_{\text {total }}=2 \mathrm{~m}$, and the breadth is $\mathrm{b}=1 \mathrm{~m}$.

The thin vertical insulating partition divides the container into two equal chambers, so each chamber has a length of $\mathrm{L}_0=1 \mathrm{~m}$ and a breadth of $\mathrm{b}=1 \mathrm{~m}$.

Each chamber contains two stacked layers of media, liquid at the bottom and air/vacuum at the top, which act as two capacitors connected in series.

The capacitance of a general two-layer vertical chamber of floor area $\mathrm{A}_{\text {floor }}=\mathrm{L}_0 \times \mathrm{b}=1 \mathrm{~m}^2$.

If the liquid of dielectric constant $\varepsilon_r$ fills the chamber up to a height $h$, the remaining height $(H-h)$ is occupied by air $(\varepsilon_r=1)$.

The capacitance of the liquid layer is:

$$C_l=\frac{\varepsilon_r\,\varepsilon_0\,A_{\mathrm{floor}}}{h}$$

The capacitance of the air layer above it is:

$$C_a=\frac{1\cdot\varepsilon_0\,A_{\mathrm{floor}}}{H-h}$$

Since these two layers are stacked on top of each other between the main plates, they are in series.

The equivalent capacitance $C_C$

$$\frac{1}{C_C}=\frac{1}{C_l}+\frac{1}{C_a}=\frac{h}{\varepsilon_r\,\varepsilon_0\,A_{\mathrm{floor}}}+\frac{H-h}{\varepsilon_0\,A_{\mathrm{floor}}}$$

$$=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0\,A_{\mathrm{floor}}}\left(\frac{h}{\varepsilon_r}+H-h\right)$$

$$C_C=\frac{\varepsilon_0A_{\mathrm{floor}}}{H-h\left(1-\frac{1}{\varepsilon_r}\right)}$$

Given that $A_{\mathrm{floor}}=1\ \mathrm{m}^2$ and $H=2\ \mathrm{m}$, the capacitance of chamber filled to a height $h$ is,

$$C(h)=\frac{\varepsilon_0}{2-h\left(1-\frac{1}{\varepsilon_r}\right)}$$

At $t=0$, the left chamber is completely filled with liquid $(h_1=2\ \mathrm{m},\ \varepsilon_r=15)$ and the right chamber is completely empty $(h_2=0\ \mathrm{m},\ \varepsilon_r=1)$.

The capacitance of left chamber at $t=0$ is

$C_{\text {left }, 0}=C(2)$ with $\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}=15$

$$ \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{left}, 0}=\frac{\varepsilon_0}{2-2\left(1-\frac{1}{15}\right)}=\frac{\varepsilon_0}{2-2\left(\frac{14}{15}\right)}=\frac{\varepsilon_0}{2-28 / 15}=\frac{\varepsilon_0}{2 / 15}=7.5 \varepsilon_0 $$

The capacitance of right chamber at $\mathrm{t}=0 \mathrm{~s}$ is (since it contains only air $\mathrm{h}_2=0$ ):

$$ \mathrm{C}_{\text {right }, 0}=\frac{\varepsilon_0}{2-0}=0.5 \varepsilon_0 $$

So, the total initial capacitance is the parallel sum of both chambers:

$$ \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{t}=0}=\mathrm{C}_{\text {left }, 0}+\mathrm{C}_{\text {right }, 0}=7.5 \varepsilon_0+0.5 \varepsilon_0=8 \varepsilon_0 $$

The height of the liquid in the left chamber at $\mathrm{t}=500 \mathrm{~s}$ is $\mathrm{h}_1=1.25 \mathrm{~m}$.

Since the total liquid mass is conserved, the height of the liquid in the right chamber is:

$$ \mathrm{h}_2=2-\mathrm{h}_1=2-1.25=0.75 \mathrm{~m} $$

The capacitance of left chamber at $\mathrm{t}=500 \mathrm{~s}$ is

$$ \mathrm{C}_{\text {left }, 500}=\frac{\varepsilon_0}{2-1.25\left(1-\frac{1}{15}\right)}=\frac{\varepsilon_0}{2-\frac{5}{4}\left(\frac{14}{15}\right)}=\frac{\varepsilon_0}{2-\frac{7}{6}}=\frac{\varepsilon_0}{5 / 6}=1.2 \varepsilon_0 $$

The capacitance of right chamber at $\mathrm{t}=500 \mathrm{~s}$ where $\mathrm{h}_2=0.75 \mathrm{~m}$ with dielectric constant $\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}=15$ :

$$C_{\text{right},500}=\frac{\varepsilon_0}{2-0.75\left(1-\frac{1}{15}\right)}=\frac{\varepsilon_0}{2-34\left(\frac{14}{15}\right)}=\frac{\varepsilon_0}{2-\frac{7}{10}}=\frac{\varepsilon_0}{13/10}=\frac{10}{13}\varepsilon_0\approx0.7692\varepsilon_0$$

So, the total capacitance at $t=500\ \mathrm{s}$ is:

$$C_{t=500}=C_{\text{left},500}+C_{\text{right},500}=1.2\varepsilon_0+\frac{10}{13}\varepsilon_0=$$

$$C_{t=500}=\frac{6}{5}\varepsilon_0+\frac{10}{13}\varepsilon_0=\frac{78+50}{65}\varepsilon_0=\frac{128}{65}\varepsilon_0\approx1.9692\varepsilon_0$$

The difference in capacitance between $t=0$ and $t=500\ \mathrm{s}$ is given by the expression $(8-n)\varepsilon_0$:

$$\Delta\ C=C_{t=0}-C_{t=500}=(8-n)\varepsilon_0$$

$$8\varepsilon_0-\frac{128}{65}\varepsilon_0=8\varepsilon_0-n\varepsilon_0$$

$$n=\frac{128}{65}\approx2$$

Therefore, the correct answer is 2.

questions.questionText: As shown in the figure, the resistance of a galvanometer G can be found by the half-deflection method. Here the resistance R2 is adjusted such that when the key K is closed the deflection in the galvanometer becomes half of the value as compared to when K is open. Half-deflection is obtained at R2 = 4 $\Omega$ and thus the galvanometer resistance is found to be 6 $\Omega$. In this half-deflection condition the current (in mA) through the resistor R1 is :
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
When the shunt key K is open, the shunt resistor $\mathrm{R}_2$ is disconnected from the circuit, which means there is no current flows through that branch. The main resistor $R_1$ and the galvanometer of resistance $G$ are connected directly in series across the constant DC voltage source V $=10 \mathrm{~V}$.

The total equivalent resistance $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq} 1}$ of this series circuit is:

$$ \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq} 1}=\mathrm{R}_1+\mathrm{G} $$

Using Ohm's law, the initial full current $\mathrm{I}_0$ flowing out of the battery and passing entirely through the galvanometer is:

$$ I_0=\frac{V}{R_1+G} $$

The angular deflection $\theta_0$ of a moving-coil galvanometer pointer is directly proportional to the current passing through its coil $\mathrm{I}_0=\mathrm{k} \theta_0$, where k is the figure of merit of the galvanometer:

$$ \theta_0 \propto \mathrm{I}_0 \Rightarrow \theta_0 \propto \frac{\mathrm{~V}}{\mathrm{R}_1+\mathrm{G}} $$

When the key K is closed, the shunt resistor $\mathrm{R}_2$ is connected in parallel across the galvanometer G . This parallel combination is connected in series with the main resistor $R_1$.

The equivalent resistance $R_p$ of the parallel section containing G and $R_2$ is:

$$R_p = \frac{GR_2}{G + R_2}$$

The new total equivalent resistance $R_{eq2}$ connected across the battery is:

$$R_{eq2} = R_1 + R_p = R_1 + \frac{GR_2}{G + R_2}$$

Using Ohm's law, the total current $I_1$ drawn from the battery which passes entirely through the series resistor $R_1$ is:

$$I_1 = \frac{V}{R_{eq2}} = \frac{V}{R_1 + \frac{GR_2}{G + R_2}}$$

When this total current $I_1$ reaches the parallel junction, it splits between the galvanometer and the shunt branch.

By the current divider rule, the fraction of current that passes through the galvanometer branch ($I_G$) is inversely proportional to its resistance:

$$I_G = I_1 \left(\frac{R_2}{G + R_2}\right)$$

Adjusting $R_2$ makes the new galvanometer deflection exactly half of its original value $(\theta = \frac{\theta_0}{2})$.

Since current and deflection are linearly proportional, the current through the galvanometer must also be halved:

$$I_G = \frac{I_0}{2}$$

Substituting expressions for $I_G$ and $I_0$ into this relationship:

$$I_1 \left(\frac{R_2}{G + R_2}\right) = \frac{1}{2} \left(\frac{V}{R_1 + G}\right)$$

$$\left(\frac{V}{R_1 + \frac{GR_2}{G + R_2}}\right) \left(\frac{R_2}{G + R_2}\right) = \frac{V}{2(R_1 + G)}$$

$$\frac{VR_2}{R_1(G + R_2) + GR_2} = \frac{V}{2(R_1 + G)}$$

$$2\,R_2(R_1 + G) = R_1(G + R_2) + GR_2$$

$$2R_1R_2 + 2GR_2 = R_1G + R_1R_2 + GR_2$$

$$2R_1R_2 - R_1R_2 + 2GR_2 - GR_2 = R_1G$$

$$R_1R_2 + GR_2 = R_1G$$

$$GR_2 = R_1G - R_1R_2 = R_1(G - R_2)$$

$$G = \frac{R_1R_2}{R_1 - R_2}$$

The galvanometer resistance is $G = 6\ \Omega$

The shunt resistance at half-deflection, $R_2 = 4\ \Omega$

$$6 = \frac{R_1\,4}{R_1 - 4}$$

$$6(R_1 - 4) = 4R_1$$

$$6R_1 - 24 = 4R_1$$

$$2R_1 = 24 \rightarrow R_1 = 12\ \Omega$$

In the half-deflection condition (with key K closed), the total current passing through resistor $R_1$ is the total circuit current $I_1$.

The parallel section resistance is,

$$R_p = \frac{GR_2}{G + R_2} = \frac{6 \times 4}{6 + 4} = \frac{24}{10} = 2.4\ \Omega$$

The total circuit resistance is,

$$R_{eq2} = R_1 + R_p = 12 + 2.4 = 14.4\ \Omega$$

The current $I_1$ from the voltage supply $V = 10\ \text{V}$:

$$I_1 = \frac{V}{R_{eq2}} = \frac{10}{14.4} = \frac{25}{36}\ \text{A}$$

$$I_1 = \frac{25}{36} \times 1000\ \text{mA} = \frac{25000}{36}\ \text{mA} \approx 694.44\ \text{mA}$$

Therefore, the correct answer is 694.44.

questions.questionText:
Two thin wires, Wire-1 of diameter 0.650 mm and Wire-2 of unknown diameter d are given. To obtain the value of d, the diameters of the two wires are measured with a screw gauge. The screw gauge has a pitch of 0.5 mm and there are 100 divisions on the circular scale (CS). The smallest division on the linear scale (LS) is 0.5 mm. The table shows the readings of LS and CS for the measurements. The value of d (in µm) is :

LS (mm)CS
Wire-10.542
Wire-21.595
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
A screw gauge measures small dimensions by converting rotational movement into linear along a main scale

(linear scale).

The Least Count (LC) is the smallest distance that can be measured accurately with the instrument. It represents

the linear distance by the screw when the circular scale is rotated through exactly one division:

$$\text{Least Count (LC)} = \frac{\text{Pitch of the screw gauge}}{\text{Total number of divisions on the circular scale}}$$

When a screw gauge is clamped around an object, the total measured dimension (D) is combination of the

visible reading on the main linear scale with the fractional reading from the circular scale:

$$D = \text{Linear Scale Reading (LSR)} + (\text{Circular Scale Reading (CSR)} \times \text{LC}) - \text{Zero Error (ZE)}$$

The pitch of given screw gauge is $P = 0.5\ \text{mm}$ and the total circular scale divisions is 100.

So, the least count is $$\text{LC} = \frac{0.5\ \text{mm}}{100} = 0.005\ \text{mm}$$

For Wire-1:

The linear scale reading is $\text{LSR}_1 = 0.5\ \text{mm}$

The circular scale reading is $\text{CSR}_1 = 42$

Using the measurement formula:

$$D_1 = \text{LSR}_1 + (\text{CSR}_1 \times \text{LC}) - \text{ZE}$$

$$0.650\ \text{mm} = 0.5\ \text{mm} + (42 \times 0.005\ \text{mm}) - \text{ZE}$$

$$0.650 = 0.5 + 0.210 - \text{ZE}$$

$$0.650 = 0.710 - \text{ZE}$$

$$\text{ZE} = 0.710 - 0.650 = 0.060\ \text{mm}$$

A positive zero error of $0.060\ \text{mm}$ means the instrument reads high because its circular zero mark has already

progressed past the linear index line before an object is inserted.

For Wire-2 the linear scale reading is $\text{LSR}_2 = 1.5\ \text{mm}$ and the circular scale reading is $\text{CSR}_2 = 95$

So, the unknown diameter of second wire is

$$d = \text{LSR}_2 + (\text{CSR}_2 \times \text{LC}) - \text{ZE}$$

$$d = 1.5\ \text{mm} + (95 \times 0.005\ \text{mm}) - 0.060\ \text{mm} = 1.5 + 0.475 - 0.060$$

$$d = 1.975 - 0.060 = 1.915\ \text{mm}$$

As $1\ \text{mm} = 1000\ \mu\text{m}$

$$d = 1.915 \times 1000\ \mu\text{m} = 1915\ \mu\text{m}$$

Therefore, the value of $d$ is 1915.

questions.questionText:
A container of height 2 m , length 2 m and breadth 1 m is made of insulating vertical walls and two large area horizontal metal plates ( $\mathrm{M}_1$ and $\mathrm{M}_2$ ) which extend far beyond the vertical walls in all directions. The container is partitioned into two equal chambers with a thin insulating vertical wall. The partition wall contains a small hole of cross-sectional area $\sqrt{10} \mathrm{~cm}^2$ near its bottom edge. Initially the hole is closed and the left chamber of the container is completely filled with a liquid of dielectric constant $\epsilon_r=15$ and the right chamber is empty ( $\epsilon_r=1$ ). At time $t=0$, the hole is opened and the liquid flows from the left chamber to the right chamber. In both the chambers, the space above the liquid has $\epsilon_r=1$ and is maintained at atmospheric pressure. The schematic of the container at a time $t>0$ is shown in the figure.

[Given : acceleration due to gravity is $10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$.]

The height (in m ) of the liquid in left chamber at $t=500 \mathrm{~s}$ is :
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
Let the height of the liquid in the left chamber at any arbitrary time t be $\mathrm{h}_1$, and the height of the liquid in the right chamber be $\mathrm{h}_2$.

Since the total volume of the liquid is conserved and both chambers have identical floor dimensions (each of length 1 m and breadth 1 m because the total length of 2 m is partitioned equally):

Area of each chamber floor is $\mathrm{A}_0=1 \mathrm{~m} \times 1 \mathrm{~m}=1 \mathrm{~m}^2$

The total initial volume of the liquid when the left chamber is completely full $\mathrm{h}_{\text {initial }}=2 \mathrm{~m}$ is:

$$ V_{\text {total }}=A_0 \times 2=2 \mathrm{~m}^3 $$

At any time $t$, the sum of the heights must satisfy:

$$A_0 h_1 + A_0 h_2 = V_{\text{total}} \Rightarrow h_1 + h_2 = 2 \Rightarrow h_2 = 2 - h_1$$

Let $v_1$ be the downward speed of the liquid surface in the left chamber, and $v$ be the velocity of the liquid bursting through the small hole of cross-sectional area $a = \sqrt{10}\ \mathrm{cm}^2 = \sqrt{10} \times 10^{-4}\ \mathrm{m}^2$.

By the principle of continuity:

$$A_0 v_1 = a v \Rightarrow v_1 = \left(\frac{a}{A_0}\right) v$$

Applying Bernoulli's equation along a streamline connecting the top surface of the left chamber to the emerging stream at the bottom hole.

Since both chambers are open to atmospheric pressure $P_0$ at the top, the pressure at both upper boundaries is equal:

$$P_0 + \rho g h_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 = P_{\text{hole}} + 0 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2$$

The static pressure at the depth of the hole from the right side filled to a height $h_2$ is

$$P_{\text{hole}} = P_0 + \rho g h_2$$

Substituting this into Bernoulli's relation:

$$P_0 + \rho g h_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 = \left(P_0 + \rho g h_2\right) + \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2$$

$$\rho g (h_1 - h_2) = \frac{1}{2}\rho (v^2 - v_1^2)$$

Substituting $v_1 = \left(\frac{a}{A_0}\right) v$:

$$2g(h_1 - h_2) = v^2 \left(1 - \frac{a^2}{A_0^2}\right)$$

Since the hole area $a$ is tiny compared to the chamber area $A_0$ so $\left(\frac{a^2}{A_0^2} \approx 0\right)$,

$$v = \sqrt{2g(h_1 - h_2)}$$

The rate of decrease of the liquid height in the left chamber is related to the flow velocity out of the hole by:

$$ -\frac{\mathrm{dh}_1}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{v}_1=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{~A}_0} \mathrm{v} $$

Substituting expressions for v and $\mathrm{h}_2=2-\mathrm{h}_1$ :

$$ -\frac{\mathrm{dh}_1}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{~A}_0} \sqrt{2 \mathrm{~g}\left(\mathrm{~h}_1-\left(2-\mathrm{h}_1\right)\right)}=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{~A}_0} \sqrt{2 \mathrm{~g}\left(2 \mathrm{~h}_1-2\right)}=\frac{2 \mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{~A}_0} \sqrt{\mathrm{~g}\left(\mathrm{~h}_1-1\right)} $$

$$ \frac{\mathrm{dh}_1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{~h}_1-1}}=-\frac{2 \mathrm{a} \sqrt{\mathrm{~g}}}{\mathrm{~A}_0} \mathrm{dt} $$

At $\mathrm{t}=0$, the left chamber is completely full, so $\mathrm{h}_1=2 \mathrm{~m}$. At time $\mathrm{t}=500 \mathrm{~s}$, let the height be $\mathrm{h}_1$ :

$$ \int_2^{\mathrm{h}_1}\left(\mathrm{~h}_1-1\right)^{-1 / 2} \mathrm{dh}_1=-\frac{2 \mathrm{a} \sqrt{\mathrm{~g}}}{\mathrm{~A}_0} \int_0^{500} \mathrm{dt} $$

$\left[2 \sqrt{\mathrm{~h}_1-1}\right]_2^{\mathrm{h}_1}=-\frac{2 \mathrm{a} \sqrt{\mathrm{g}}}{\mathrm{A}_0}[\mathrm{t}]_0^{500}$

$2 \sqrt{\mathrm{~h}_1-1}-2 \sqrt{2-1}=-\frac{2 \mathrm{a} \sqrt{\mathrm{g}}}{\mathrm{A}_0}(500)$

$$ \sqrt{\mathrm{h}_1-1}-1=-\frac{\mathrm{a} \sqrt{\mathrm{~g}}}{\mathrm{~A}_0}(500) $$

The area of hole is $\mathrm{a}=\sqrt{10} \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2$, the area of each chamber is $A_0=1 \mathrm{~m}^2$

$$ \sqrt{\mathrm{h}_1-1}-1=\frac{\sqrt{10} \times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{10}}{1} \times 500=10 \times 10^{-4} \times 500=10^{-3} \times 500=0.5 $$

$$\sqrt{h_1 - 1} - 1 = -0.5$$

$$\sqrt{h_1 - 1} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5$$

$$h_1 - 1 = (0.5)^2 = 0.25$$

$$h_1 = 1 + 0.25 = 1.25\ \mathrm{m}$$

Therefore, the correct answer is 1.25.

questions.questionText:
A uniform circular disk of radius 0.2 m and mass 1 kg is pivoted at its top point $C$ such that it can rotate freely around $C$ in the $X Y$ plane, as shown in the figure. Initially, when the disk is at rest, a particle of mass 20 g , travelling along negative $x$ direction in the $X Y$ plane with speed $100 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$, hits the circumference of the disk at a point $P$. After collision the particle moves along negative $y$ direction at a speed of $90 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$.

[Given : the acceleration due to gravity $(\mathrm{g})=-10 \hat{\jmath} \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$ ]

After the collision the disk starts to rotate around point $C$ in the $X Y$ plane. The maximum change in the height (in m ) of its center $O$ is :
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
Relative to a coordinate system centered at the disk's center $O(0,0)$ when it hangs vertically at rest.

The disk has radius $R = 0.2\ \mathrm{m}$ and mass $M = 1\ \mathrm{kg}$.

It is pivoted at its top point $C$. In this orientation, $C$ is at coordinates $(0,R) = (0,0.2\ \mathrm{m})$.

The impact point $P$ lies on the circumference such that the radius $OP$ makes an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with vertical.

The position vector of the collision point $P$ relative to the pivot $C$:

$$\vec{r}_{P/C} = \vec{r}_P - \vec{r}_C$$

The position of the center $O$ relative to pivot $C$ is:

$$\vec{r}_{O/C} = -R\hat{\jmath} = -0.2\hat{\jmath}$$

Point $P$ is located in the $4^{\mathrm{th}}$ quadrant at an angle of $45^{\circ}$ relative to the $\hat{\imath}$ axis:

$$\vec{r}_{P/O} = R\cos(45^{\circ})\hat{\imath} - R\sin(45^{\circ})\hat{\jmath} = 0.2 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\hat{\imath} - 0.2 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\hat{\jmath}$$

Thus, the vector from the pivot $C$ to the impact point $P$ is:

$$\vec{r}_{P/C} = \vec{r}_{P/O} - \vec{r}_{C/O} = \left(0.2 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\hat{\imath} - 0.2 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\hat{\jmath}\right) - (0.2\hat{\jmath}) = 0.2 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\hat{\imath} - \left(0.2 + 0.2 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\hat{\jmath}$$

During the instantaneous collision, gravity is a non-impulsive force, and the reaction forces at the pivot $C$ exert zero torque about $C$. Therefore, the total angular momentum $\vec{L}_C$ of the system about the pivot point $C$ is strictly conserved.

$$\vec{L}_{C,\mathrm{initial}} = \vec{L}_{C,\mathrm{final}}$$

The colliding particle of mass $m = 20\ \mathrm{g} = 0.02\ \mathrm{kg}$:

Before impact the particle travels along the negative x-direction with speed $v_1 = 100\ \mathrm{m\ s^{-1}}$.

$$\vec{V}_1 = -100\hat{\imath}$$

After impact the particle travels along the negative y-direction with speed $v_2 = 90 ext{ m s}^{-1}$

$$\vec{v}_2 = -90\hat{\jmath}$$

The disk is initially stationary, so only the particle contributes to angular momentum:

$$\vec{L}_{C,\text{initial}} = \vec{r}_{P/C} \times (m\vec{v}_1)$$

$$\vec{L}_{C,\text{initial}} = \left[0.2 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\hat{\imath} - \left(0.2 + 0.2 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\hat{\jmath}\right] \times \left[0.02(-100\hat{\imath})\right]$$

$$\vec{L}_{C,\text{initial}} = \left[\frac{0.2}{\sqrt{2}}\hat{\imath} - \left(0.2 + \frac{0.2}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\hat{\jmath}\right] \times \left[-2\hat{\imath}\right]$$

Since $\hat{\imath} \times \hat{\imath} = 0$ and $\hat{\jmath} \times \hat{\imath} = -\hat{k}$:

$$\vec{L}_{C,\text{initial}} = -\left(0.2 + \frac{0.2}{\sqrt{2}}\right)(-2)(-\hat{k}) = -2\left(0.2 + \frac{0.2}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\hat{k} = -\left(0.4 + \frac{0.4}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\hat{k}$$

Right after the collision, the particle has velocity $\vec{v}_2$ and the disk rotates around $C$ with an angular velocity $\omega$:

$$\vec{L}_{C,\text{final}} = \left[\vec{r}_{P/C} \times (m\vec{v}_2)\right] + I_C\,\omega\hat{k}$$

$$\vec{r}_{P/C} \times (m\vec{v}_2) = \left[\frac{0.2}{\sqrt{2}}\hat{\imath} - \left(0.2 + \frac{0.2}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\hat{\jmath}\right] \times \left[0.02(-90\hat{\jmath})\right]$$

$$\vec{r}_{P/C} \times (m\vec{v}_2) = \left[\frac{0.2}{\sqrt{2}}\hat{\imath} - \left(0.2 + \frac{0.2}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\hat{\jmath}\right] \times \left[-1.8\hat{\jmath}\right]$$

Since $\hat{\imath} \times \hat{\jmath} = \hat{k}$ and $\hat{\jmath} \times \hat{\jmath} = 0$:

$$\vec{r}_{P/C} \times (m\vec{v}_2) = \left(\frac{0.2}{\sqrt{2}}\right)(-1.8)\hat{k} = -\frac{0.36}{\sqrt{2}}\hat{k}$$

By the Parallel Axis Theorem, the moment of inertia $I_C$ of the uniform disk about its rim pivot point $C$ is:

$$I_C = I_{\text{cm}} + MR^2 = \frac{1}{2}MR^2 + MR^2 = \frac{3}{2}MR^2$$

$$I_C = \frac{3}{2}(1)(0.2)^2 = \frac{3}{2} \times 0.04 = 0.06\text{ kg m}^2$$

Equating the initial and final angular momentum expressions:

$$\left(0.4 + \frac{0.4}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\hat{k} = \frac{0.36}{\sqrt{2}}\hat{k} + 0.06\omega\hat{k}$$

$$0.4 + \frac{0.4}{\sqrt{2}} - \frac{0.36}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.06\omega$$

$$0.4 + \frac{0.04}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.06\omega$$

$$0.4 + \frac{0.04}{1.414} \approx 0.4 + 0.0283 = 0.4283$$

$$ \omega=\frac{0.4283}{0.06} \approx 7.138 \mathrm{rad} \mathrm{~s}^{-1} $$

Immediately after the collision, the newly acquired kinetic energy of the spinning disk is purely rotational about the fixed pivot C :

$$ \begin{gathered} \mathrm{K}_{\text {disk }}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{C}} \omega^2 \\ \mathrm{~K}_{\text {disk }}=\frac{1}{2}(0.06)(7.138)^2=0.03 \times 50.95=1.5285 \mathrm{~J} \end{gathered} $$

As the disk swings upwards around the pivot point C , its kinetic energy is gradually converted entirely into gravitational potential energy. The disk reaches its maximum displacement height when it momentarily comes to a complete rest $(K=0)$.

Let $\Delta h$ be the maximum vertical upward displacement of the disk's center of mass O:

$$\Delta K + \Delta U = 0 \rightarrow K_{\text{disk}} = Mg\,\Delta h$$

Substitute the known values $M = 1\ \text{kg}$ and $g = 10\ \text{m s}^{-2}$

$$1.5285 = (1) \times (10) \times \Delta h$$

$$\Delta h = \frac{1.5285}{10} = 0.15285\ \text{m} \approx 0.15\ \text{m}$$

Therefore, the correct answer is $0.15$.

questions.questionText:
A uniform circular disk of radius 0.2 m and mass 1 kg is pivoted at its top point $C$ such that it can rotate freely around $C$ in the $X Y$ plane, as shown in the figure. Initially, when the disk is at rest, a particle of mass 20 g , travelling along negative $x$ direction in the $X Y$ plane with speed $100 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$, hits the circumference of the disk at a point $P$. After collision the particle moves along negative $y$ direction at a speed of $90 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$.

[Given : the acceleration due to gravity $(\mathrm{g})=-10 \hat{\jmath} \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$ ]

Amount of energy loss (in J ) in the collision is :
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
Before the collision, the disk is entirely at rest. Therefore, the total initial kinetic energy of the system is carried only by the translation of the incoming particle.

The kinetic energy of a moving particle of mass $m$ with speed $v$ is $K = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$

Given values for the particle before impact the mass is $m = 20\ \mathrm{g} = 0.02\ \mathrm{kg}$ and its initial velocity, $v_1 = 100\ \mathrm{m\ s^{-1}}$

Substituting these values into the kinetic energy equation:

$$K_{\mathrm{initial}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.02\ \mathrm{kg} \times (100\ \mathrm{m\ s^{-1}})^2$$

$$K_{\mathrm{initial}} = 0.01 \times 10000 = 100\ \mathrm{J}$$

Immediately after the collision, the total kinetic energy of the system is the sum of the translational kinetic energy of the scattering particle and the rotational kinetic energy of the spinning disk about its fixed pivot C.

$$K_{\mathrm{final}} = K_{\mathrm{particle,final}} + K_{\mathrm{disk,final}}$$

For the particle after collision, final velocity is $v_2 = 90\ \mathrm{m\ s^{-1}}$

$$K_{\text{particle,final}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.02\ \mathrm{kg} \times \left(90\ \mathrm{m\ s^{-1}}\right)^2$$

$$K_{\text{particle,final}} = 0.01 \times 8100 = 81\ \mathrm{J}$$

The disk rotates around point C with an angular velocity of $\omega \approx 7.138\ \mathrm{rad\ s^{-1}}$.

Using the moment of inertia about the rim pivot C ($I_C = 0.06\ \mathrm{kg\ m^2}$), the rotational kinetic energy component is:

$$K_{\text{disk,final}} = \frac{1}{2} I_C\,\omega^2$$

$$K_{\text{disk,final}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.06 \times (7.138)^2 \approx 1.53\ \mathrm{J}$$

So, the final kinetic energy of system is,

$$K_{\text{final}} = 81\ \mathrm{J} + 1.53\ \mathrm{J} = 82.53\ \mathrm{J}$$

Energy Loss = $K_{\text{initial}} - K_{\text{final}}$

Energy Loss = $100\ \mathrm{J} - 82.53\ \mathrm{J} = 17.47\ \mathrm{J}$

Therefore, the correct answer is 17.47.

questions.questionText:
In a single slit diffraction experiment, a slit of width $(0.016 \pm 0.002)$ mm is used to measure the wavelength of a monochromatic light source. In the diffraction pattern, the angular distance between the central maximum and first minimum is measured to be $(2^{\circ} \pm 40')$. The value of the fractional error in the measurement of wavelength is :

[Given : $\sin(2^{\circ}) = 0.035$]
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
Let the monochromatic light is of wavelength $\lambda$ incident normally on a single long slit of width $a$.

When wavelets are diffracted at an angle $\theta$, a path difference is introduced between waves originating from different parts of the slit.

The path difference between a wavelet originating from the top edge of the slit and one originating from the exact midpoint of the slit is:

$$\Delta x = \frac{a}{2} \sin \theta$$

For the first destructive interference to occur on the screen, this path difference must equal exactly half a wavelength $\left(\frac{\lambda}{2}\right)$, so that wavelets from the upper half completely cancel wavelets from the lower half out of phase:

$$\frac{a}{2} \sin \theta = \frac{\lambda}{2} \rightarrow a \sin \theta = \lambda$$

$$\lambda = a \sin \theta$$

So, the value of wavelength $\lambda$ depends on two independent measurements, the slit width $a$ and the diffraction angle $\theta$.

Taking the natural logarithm $(\ln)$ on both sides

$$\ln \lambda = \ln(a \sin \theta) = \ln a + \ln(\sin \theta)$$

Differentiating both sides:

$$\frac{d\lambda}{\lambda} = \frac{da}{a} + \frac{d(\sin \theta)}{\sin \theta}$$

$$\frac{d\lambda}{\lambda} = \frac{da}{a} + \frac{\cos \theta\, d\theta}{\sin \theta} = \frac{da}{a} + (\cot \theta) d\theta$$

For calculating the maximum possible relative or fractional error, absolute errors are added together:

$$\frac{\Delta \lambda}{\lambda} = \frac{\Delta a}{a} + (\cot \theta) \Delta \theta$$

The slit width is $a = 0.016\ \text{mm}$ with error $\Delta a = 0.002\ \text{mm}$

$$\frac{\Delta a}{a} = \frac{0.002\ \text{mm}}{0.016\ \text{mm}} = \frac{2}{16} = 0.125$$

The angular position is $\theta = 2^{\circ}$ with error $\Delta \theta = 40' = \left(\frac{40}{60}\right)^{\circ} = \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{\circ}$

$$\theta_{\text{rad}} = 2 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = \frac{\pi}{90}\ \text{rad}$$

So, $$\Delta \theta_{\text{rad}} = \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{\pi}{180} = \frac{2\pi}{540} = \frac{\pi}{270}\ \text{rad}$$

Using the given approximation $\sin(2^{\circ}) = 0.035$:

$$\cos(2^{\circ}) = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2(2^{\circ})} = \sqrt{1 - (0.035)^2} = \sqrt{1 - 0.001225} = \sqrt{0.998775} \approx 1$$

Therefore, the value of $\cot(2^{\circ})$ is:

$$\cot(2^{\circ}) = \frac{\cos(2^{\circ})}{\sin(2^{\circ})} \approx \frac{1}{0.035} = 2007$$

So, fractional change in measurement of angular position,

$$(\cot \theta)\Delta \theta = (2007) \times \left(\frac{\pi}{270}\right) = \frac{20\pi}{189}$$

$$(\cot \theta)\Delta \theta \approx \frac{20 \times 3.14}{189} = \frac{62.832}{189} \approx 0.332$$

So, the fractional change in wavelength is

$$\frac{\Delta \lambda}{\lambda} = 0.125 + 0.332 = 0.457 \approx 0.46$$

Therefore, the fractional error in the measurement of wavelength is $0.46$.

questions.questionText: In a new system of units, the units of mass, length, time and current are $5 \mathrm{~kg}, 5 \mathrm{~m}, 5 \mathrm{~s}$ and 5 A , respectively. If $\mu_0$ and $\epsilon_0$ are the permeability and permittivity of free space, respectively, then in this new system of units, the magnitude of one SI unit of $\sqrt{\mu_0 / \epsilon_0}$, is :
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
The quantity $\sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{\epsilon_0}}$ is known as the characteristic impedance of free space ($Z_0$).

Using the relationship between the speed of light $c$, permeability $\mu_0$, and permittivity $\epsilon_0$:

$$c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \epsilon_0}} \rightarrow \mu_0\,\epsilon_0 = \frac{1}{c^2} \rightarrow \mu_0 = \frac{1}{c^2\,\epsilon_0}$$

Substituting this for $\mu_0$ into $\sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{\epsilon_0}}$

$$\sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{\epsilon_0}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{c^2\,\epsilon_0\epsilon_0}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{c^2\,\epsilon_0^2}} = \frac{1}{c\,\epsilon_0}$$

The dimensional formula of permittivity $\epsilon_0$ can be determined using Coulomb's Law.

The electrostatic force $F$ between two point charges $q_1$ and $q_2$ separated by a distance $r$ is:

$$F = \frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0}\frac{q_1\,q_2}{r^2} \rightarrow \epsilon_0 = \frac{1}{4\pi F}\frac{q_1\,q_2}{r^2}$$

Electric charge $q$ is defined as current $(I)$ multiplied by time $(T)$, so its dimensional formula is $[A\ T]$.

Force has dimensions of mass times acceleration, $[M\ L\ T^{-2}]$.

Distance $r$ has the dimension of length $[L]$.

Substituting these definitions gives the dimensional formula for $\epsilon_0$:

$$[\epsilon_0] = \frac{[A\ T][A\ T]}{[M\ L\ T^{-2}][L^2]} = \frac{[A^2\ T^2]}{[M\ L^3\ T^{-2}]} = [M^{-1}\ L^{-3}\ T^4\ A^2]$$

The speed of light $c$ is a velocity, which has the dimensional formula $[L\ T^{-1}]$.

Now, substituting the dimensions of $c$ and $\epsilon_0$ into our expression $Z_0 = \frac{1}{c\epsilon_0}$:

$$Z_0 = \left[\sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{\epsilon_0}}\right] = \frac{1}{[L\ T^{-1}][M^{-1}\ L^{-3}\ T^4\ A^2]} = \frac{1}{[M^{-1}\ L^{-2}\ T^3\ A^2]} = [M^1\ L^2\ T^{-3}\ A^{-2}]$$

Let the unit of the quantity in the standard SI system be $U_1$ and its numerical magnitude be $N_1$.

Let its unit in the new system be $U_2$ and its numerical magnitude be $N_2$.

Since the physical value remains identical regardless of the unit system chosen:

$$N_1\,U_1 = N_2\,U_2 \Rightarrow N_2 = N_1\left(\frac{U_1}{U_2}\right)$$

Using dimensional formula $[M^1\ L^2\ T^{-3}\ A^{-2}]$, we can express the ratio of the units as:

$$N_2 = N_1\left[\frac{M_1}{M_2}\right]^1\left[\frac{L_1}{L_2}\right]^2\left[\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right]^{-3}\left[\frac{A_1}{A_2}\right]^{-2}$$

Where $M_1, L_1, T_1, A_1$ represent the base units in the standard SI system $(1 { kg}, 1 { m}, 1 { s}, 1 { A})$. And $M_2, L_2, T_2, A_2$ represent the base units in the new system $(5 { kg}, 5 { m}, 5 { s}, 5 { A})$.

The magnitude in the new system that corresponds to exactly one SI unit of the quantity.

$$N_1 = 1$$

Substituting the standard SI values and the given new system values into the conversion equation:

$$N_2 = 1 \times \left[\frac{1\text{ kg}}{5\text{kg}}\right]^1 \left[\frac{1\text{ m}}{5\text{ m}}\right]^2 \left[\frac{1\text{ s}}{5\text{ s}}\right]^{-3} \left[\frac{1\text{ A}}{5\text{ A}}\right]^{-2}$$

$$N_2 = \left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^1 \times \left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^2 \times \left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^{-3} \times \left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^{-2}$$

$$N_2 = \left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^{1+2-3-2}$$

$$N_2 = \left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^{-2}$$

$$N_2 = (5)^2 = 25$$

Therefore, the correct answer is 25.

questions.questionText: As shown in the figure, a ray AB of unpolarized light enters from water of refractive index $n_w = \frac{4}{3}$ into a medium of refractive index $n_p = \frac{4}{\sqrt{3}}$ after passing through a glass plate of refractive index $n_g = 1.5$ and a layer of water. At a particular incident angle $i$ the reflected ray CD is polarized in the direction as shown in the figure. The value of $i$ (in degrees) is :
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
When unpolarized light is incident on a boundary separating two optical media with different refractive indices, a part is reflected and the remaining is refracted.

The electric field vectors in unpolarized light oscillate randomly across all planes perpendicular to its direction of travel, parallel $(\downarrow)$ to the plane of incidence and perpendicular to the plane of incidence $(\odot)$ (parallel to the reflecting surface).

The reflected ray is composed entirely of oscillations perpendicular to the plane of incidence, making it completely linearly polarized.

When the angle between the reflected and refracted rays is $90^{\circ}$ :

$$ \mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{p}}+90^{\circ}+\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{p}}=180^{\circ} \Rightarrow \mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{p}}+\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{p}}=90^{\circ} \Rightarrow \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{p}}=90^{\circ}-\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{p}} $$

Applying Snell's law when light is moving from a medium of index $\mathrm{n}_1$ to a medium of index $\mathrm{n}_2$ ):

$$ \mathrm{n}_1 \sin \mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{p}}=\mathrm{n}_2 \sin \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{p}} $$

Substituting $\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{p}}=90^{\circ}-\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{p}}$ into the equation:

$$ \mathrm{n}_1 \sin \mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{p}}=\mathrm{n}_2 \sin \left(90^{\circ}-\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{p}}\right)=\mathrm{n}_2 \cos \mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{p}} $$

$$ \frac{\sin \mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\cos \mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{p}}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_2}{\mathrm{n}_1} \Rightarrow \tan \mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{p}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_2}{\mathrm{n}_1} $$

This relationship is known as Brewster's Law.

Reflection occurs at the final boundary where light tries to pass from the water layer underneath the prism medium into the lower medium $\left(\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{p}}\right)$.

Initial medium is water layer above the glass is $\mathrm{n}_1=\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{w}}=\frac{4}{3}$

The final medium is of refractive index $\mathrm{n}_2=\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{p}}=\frac{4}{\sqrt{3}}$

Let $\theta$ be the angle of incidence at this bottom water-prism boundary.

Applying Brewster's Law:

$$ \tan \theta=\frac{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{w}}}=\frac{4 / \sqrt{3}}{4 / 3}=\frac{3}{\sqrt{3}}=\sqrt{3} $$

Since $\tan \left(60^{\circ}\right)=\sqrt{3}$, the internal angle of incidence at this interface must be:

$$ \theta=60^{\circ} $$

Applying Snell's law sequentially at each boundary interface:

Boundary 1: $n_w \, \sin i = n_g \, \sin r_g$

Boundary 2: $n_g \, \sin r_g = n_w \, \sin r_w$

Boundary 3: $n_w \, \sin r_w = n_p \, \sin r_p$

So, $n_w \, \sin i = n_w \, \sin \theta$

Where $r_w = \theta = 60^\circ$.

$\sin i = \sin \theta$

$\sin i = \sin(60^\circ) \Rightarrow i = 60^\circ$

Therefore, the correct answer is 60.
